sentences.base_language: Yamamoto Yuki is a student who is about to graduate and is looking for work.
sentences.target_language: 山本ゆきは学生で、もうすぐ卒業するので、仕事を探しています。
sentences.romanized: Yamamoto Yuki wa gakusei de, mōsugu sotsugyō suru node, shigoto wo sagashite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**もうすぐ** = soon / before long: もうすぐ卒業する (graduating soon). **もう** alone means 'already': もう終わった (already finished). Combined with **すぐ** (right away/soon), it expresses imminent action.
**〜ので** = because (explanatory/causal): 卒業するので仕事を探している (because she's graduating, she's looking for work). **ので** is slightly more formal and softer than **から**. Both mean 'because' but **ので** gives a factual, objective reason; **から** can feel more assertive or subjective.

sentences.base_language: Today she has an interview for an engineer position and feels very nervous.
sentences.target_language: 今日、エンジニアの仕事の面接があって、とても緊張しています。
sentences.romanized: Kyō, enjinia no shigoto no mensetsu ga atte, totemo kinchō shite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜があって** = 'there is ~ and (so)': 面接があって、緊張している (there is an interview, so she is nervous). **ある** (aru) expresses the existence of things and scheduled events: 今日、授業がある (I have class today), 明日、会議があります (there is a meeting tomorrow).
**緊張する** = to be/feel nervous: **緊張** (kinchō) = tension/nervousness. **緊張しています** uses **〜ている** because the nervous state is ongoing. Compare: **緊張した** (got nervous — the moment of onset).

sentences.base_language: In the morning, she took a deep breath in front of the mirror and told herself: "No problem — I can do it."
sentences.target_language: 朝、鏡の前で深呼吸して、「大丈夫、できる」と自分に言い聞かせました。
sentences.romanized: Asa, kagami no mae de fukukokyū shite, 'Daijōbu, dekiru' to jibun ni iikikasemashita.
literal: it's okay, I can do it
自分に言い聞かせました
sentences.grammarNote:
**言い聞かせる** (iikikaseru) = to tell oneself / to persuade oneself, lit. 'to make [someone] hear by saying' — a compound verb: **言う** (say) + **聞かせる** (make listen). 自分に言い聞かせる (to tell oneself) is more nuanced than just 言う — it implies a deliberate, repeated internal coaching.
**大丈夫** (daijōbu) = all right / no problem / it's okay — an incredibly versatile word. Used to reassure (大丈夫ですよ), to decline (大丈夫です = no thanks, I'm fine), or to ask (大丈夫ですか = are you okay?).

sentences.base_language: She had eggs and bread for breakfast, and drank a glass of milk.
sentences.target_language: 朝ご飯に卵とパンを食べて、牛乳を一杯飲みました。
sentences.romanized: Asagohan ni tamago to pan wo tabete, gyūnyū wo ippai nomimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜に食べる** = eat as/for a meal: 朝ご飯に食べる (eat for breakfast). **に** here marks the purpose or occasion: 朝ご飯に (for breakfast), おやつに (as a snack), プレゼントに (as a gift).
**一杯** (ippai) = one cup/glass — a counter for drinks in cups or glasses. **一杯飲む** = have a drink (can also mean 'have a drink' socially: 一杯どう？= How about a drink?). Counters in Japanese: 一枚 (one flat thing), 一匹 (one small animal), 一本 (one long thin thing).

sentences.base_language: The office was in the city centre, and she went by subway.
sentences.target_language: オフィスは市内にあって、彼女は地下鉄で行きました。
sentences.romanized: Ofisu wa shinai ni atte, kanojo wa chikatetsu de ikimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**で** after a vehicle or means = 'by' (method of transport): 地下鉄で (by subway), バスで (by bus), 自転車で (by bicycle), 歩いて (on foot — exception: uses て-form, not で). Compare: **に乗る** = to ride/board: 地下鉄に乗る (ride the subway). You ride **に** but travel **で**.
**市内** (shinai) = city centre / within the city: **市** (shi, city) + **内** (nai/uchi, inside). Compare: **都内** (tonai, within the metropolis), **区内** (kunai, within the ward).

sentences.base_language: On the subway, her hands were sweating from nerves.
sentences.target_language: 地下鉄の中で、緊張のせいで手に汗をかいていました。
sentences.romanized: Chikatetsu no naka de, kinchō no sei de te ni ase wo kaite imashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜のせいで** = because of ~ (negative cause, blame): 緊張のせいで手汗をかく (sweating from nerves). **せい** (性/所為) implies something undesirable. Contrast: **〜のおかげで** = thanks to ~ (positive cause). The distinction is important: 雨のせいで遅れた (late because of rain — rain is the problem) vs 友達のおかげで助かった (saved thanks to my friend — grateful).
**汗をかく** = to sweat, lit. 'to produce sweat' — **かく** (kaku) has many uses in idioms: 汗をかく (sweat), 恥をかく (be embarrassed), 嘘をつく (tell a lie).

sentences.base_language: She arrived at the company building and glanced at her watch: nine-twenty.
sentences.target_language: 会社のビルに着いて、腕時計を見ると、九時二十分でした。
sentences.romanized: Kaisha no biru ni tsuite, udedokei wo miru to, kuji nijuppun deshita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**着く** (tsuku) = to arrive (at a destination) — takes the particle **に**: 駅に着く (arrive at the station), 家に着く (arrive home). Compare: **到着する** (tōchaku suru) is more formal. Also: **つく** can mean 'attach' — a homophone written with a different kanji (付く).
**〜と** (discovery conditional) = 'when I did X, I found that Y': 見ると、九時二十分だった (when I looked, it was 9:20). This **と** captures the moment of discovery. Very useful pattern: 開けると、猫がいた (when I opened it, there was a cat), 調べると、エラーがあった (when I checked, there was an error).

sentences.base_language: The receptionist said: "Welcome. How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: 受付の人が「いらっしゃいませ。どのようなご用件でしょうか」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Uketsuke no hito ga 'Irasshaimase. Dono yō na go-yōken deshō ka' to iimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**いらっしゃいませ** = welcome (formal greeting to a customer) — the polite imperative of **いらっしゃる**, the honorific for 'to be/to go/to come'. Heard constantly in Japanese shops and businesses. You do not need to reply to it.
**ご用件** (go-yōken) = the matter / what you need — **ご** is an honorific prefix (for words of Japanese origin) that elevates the meaning: ご用件 (your business), ご連絡 (your message), ご注文 (your order). **でしょうか** is the polite question form of **だ/です**: extremely polite and formal register.

sentences.base_language: Yuki replied: "Good morning. My name is Yamamoto Yuki and I have an interview at nine-thirty."
sentences.target_language: ゆきさんは「おはようございます。山本ゆきと申します。九時半に面接の予約があります」と答えました。
sentences.romanized: Yuki-san wa 'Ohayō gozaimasu. Yamamoto Yuki to mōshimasu. Kuji han ni mensetsu no yoyaku ga arimasu' to kotaemashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と申します** (to mōshimasu) = my name is ~ (humble form of **言う**). This is **keigo** (敬語, polite/formal language): **申す** (mōsu) is the humble form of **言う** (iu, to say). When introducing yourself formally: 山本と申します is more polite than 山本と言います or 山本です.
**九時半** (kuji han) = nine-thirty — **半** (han) = half: 三時半 (three-thirty), 十一時半 (eleven-thirty). For telling time: [hour]時 + [minutes]分, with **半** as a shortcut for 三十分. Note: 九時三十分 is equivalent to 九時半.

sentences.base_language: The manager, Mr. Tanaka, arrived, shook her hand, and said: "Nice to meet you."
sentences.target_language: 田中部長が来て、握手して、「はじめまして、どうぞよろしくお願いします」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Tanaka buchō ga kite, akushu shite, 'Hajimemashite, dōzo yoroshiku onegai shimasu' to iimashita.
literal: どうぞよろしくお願いします
sentences.grammarNote:
**はじめまして** = nice to meet you (first meeting), lit. 'it is the first time' — always used at a first introduction. The standard follow-up is **どうぞよろしくお願いします** (please be good to me / I look forward to working with you), which is often shortened to **よろしくお願いします** or casually **よろしく**.
**部長** (buchō) = department manager/director — a key title. Japanese companies use hierarchical titles: 社員 (employee), 係長 (section chief), 課長 (section manager), 部長 (department head), 取締役 (director), 社長 (president).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Tanaka asked her: "Why do you want to be an engineer?"
sentences.target_language: 田中部長はゆきさんに「なぜエンジニアになりたいのですか」と聞きました。
sentences.romanized: Tanaka buchō wa Yuki-san ni 'Naze enjinia ni naritai no desu ka' to kikimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜になる** = to become ~ (role, state): エンジニアになる (become an engineer), 医者になる (become a doctor). **に** marks the resulting state after **なる**. Compare: **〜である** (to be, as a formal statement of identity) vs **〜になる** (to transition into that state).
**〜たいのですか** = do you want to do? — **〜たい** (want to do) + **のですか** (explanatory question). The **の** (or **ん**) adds a nuance of 'is it the case that...?' — making the question feel more exploratory and considerate than a plain **〜たいですか**.

sentences.base_language: Yuki replied: "Because I love designing and building software to help people."
sentences.target_language: ゆきさんは「人の役に立つソフトウェアをデザインして作ることが大好きだからです」と答えました。
sentences.romanized: Yuki-san wa 'Hito no yaku ni tatsu sofutowea wo dezain shite tsukuru koto ga daisuki dakara desu' to kotaemashita.
literal: デザインして作ること
the act of designing and making
sentences.grammarNote:
**役に立つ** (yaku ni tatsu) = to be useful/helpful, lit. 'to stand in a role' — a key idiom: この情報は役に立ちます (this information is useful), 人の役に立ちたい (I want to be helpful to people).
**〜こと** nominalises a verb phrase: **デザインして作ること** = the act of designing and making (a noun phrase). **が大好き** = love very much (が marks the object of emotion: 〜が好き = like ~, 〜が大好き = love ~). **から** = because (explains the reason for the statement).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Tanaka nodded, smiled, and said: "Very good answers." He seemed very satisfied.
sentences.target_language: 田中部長はうなずいて、微笑んで、「とてもいい答えですね」と言いました。とても満足しているようでした。
sentences.romanized: Tanaka buchō wa unazuite, hohoende, 'Totemo ii kotae desu ne' to iimashita. Totemo manzoku shite iru yō deshita.
literal: とてもいい答えですね
満足しているようでした
sentences.grammarNote:
**ね** at the end of a sentence invites agreement or shared feeling: いい答えですね (great answers, aren't they). **ね** says 'I feel this, and I think you do too.' Compare **よ** (asserting information to the listener) — **ね** shares, **よ** informs.
**〜ようだ / 〜ようでした** = seems/appeared to be: 満足しているようでした (seemed satisfied). Based on observable evidence. Compare **〜そうだ**: 満足しそう (looks like he'll be satisfied — prediction from appearance) vs 満足しているようだ (seems satisfied — based on behaviour observed).

sentences.base_language: After the interview, Yuki felt much calmer.
sentences.target_language: 面接の後、ゆきさんはずっと落ち着きました。
sentences.romanized: Mensetsu no ato, Yuki-san wa zutto ochitsukimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**落ち着く** (ochitsuku) = to calm down / to settle: 落ち着いて (calm down — as a command), 落ち着いてきた (started to calm down), 落ち着いた (became calm). **落ち着き** as a noun means 'composure/calmness' — seen in the vocabulary deck.
**〜の後** (no ato) = after ~: 食事の後 (after the meal), 面接の後 (after the interview). With verbs: **〜た後で** (after having done): 食べた後で (after eating). **〜てから** (after doing, then) tends to emphasise the sequence: 食べてから行く (I'll go after eating).

sentences.base_language: She left the building and found a café nearby.
sentences.target_language: ビルを出て、近くにカフェを見つけました。
sentences.romanized: Biru wo dete, chikaku ni kafe wo mitsukemashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**見つける** (mitsukeru) = to find (with effort or discovery): カフェを見つけた (found a café). Compare **見つかる** (mitsukaru) = to be found (intransitive): 財布が見つかった (the wallet was found). Japanese often has transitive/intransitive verb pairs: 見つける/見つかる, 開ける/開く (open/be open), 始める/始まる (begin/start).
**近くに** = nearby (location): **近く** (chikaku) = the vicinity. **に** marks the location of discovery or existence: 近くにカフェがある (there is a café nearby), 近くに住んでいる (live nearby).

sentences.base_language: She ordered a coffee, sat down, and sent her mom a message.
sentences.target_language: コーヒーを注文して、席に座って、お母さんにメッセージを送りました。
sentences.romanized: Kōhī wo chūmon shite, seki ni suwatte, okāsan ni messēji wo okurimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**注文する** (chūmon suru) = to order (food/goods): コーヒーを注文する (order a coffee). In a restaurant: **ご注文はお決まりですか** (have you decided on your order? — formal service phrase).
**席に座る** = sit in/at a seat — **に** marks destination of the sitting action: 椅子に座る (sit on a chair), ソファに座る (sit on the sofa). Compare **に** for location of existence: 椅子に座っている (is sitting on a chair, resulting state).

sentences.base_language: She wrote: "Mom, the interview is done! I think it went well for me."
sentences.target_language: 「お母さん、面接が終わったよ！うまくいったと思います」と書きました。
sentences.romanized: 'Okāsan, mensetsu ga owatta yo! Umaku itta to omoimasu' to kakimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**うまくいく** = to go well / to succeed, lit. 'to go skillfully': 面接はうまくいった (the interview went well), 計画がうまくいかない (the plan isn't working out). **うまく** is the adverb from **うまい** (skilful/good). Common expression: うまくいくといいね (I hope it goes well).
**〜と思う** (to omou) = I think (that): うまくいったと思います (I think it went well). The **と** here is the quotation particle — marking what you think. Contrast with **〜と思っている** (ongoing belief) and **〜と思った** (thought at a moment in the past).

sentences.base_language: Her mom replied quickly: "How wonderful! Tonight I'll make the fish you like most."
sentences.target_language: お母さんはすぐに「よかった！今夜、あなたが一番好きな魚を作るね」と返事しました。
sentences.romanized: Okāsan wa sugu ni 'Yokatta! Kon'ya, anata ga ichiban suki na sakana wo tsukuru ne' to henji shimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**よかった** = 'how great / thank goodness!' — the past tense of **いい** (good): literally 'it was good.' Used to express relief or happy reaction: よかった！(great news! / what a relief!) — one of the most natural Japanese reactions.
**一番好きな** = most-liked / favourite: **一番** (ichiban) = number one / most + **好きな** (liked). This is the superlative pattern: 一番大きい (the biggest), 一番おいしい (the most delicious), クラスで一番速い (the fastest in the class). **ね** at the end softly shares the plan with the listener.

sentences.base_language: Yuki smiled, feeling wonderful. She sipped her coffee and looked at the street through the window.
sentences.target_language: ゆきさんは微笑んで、とても幸せな気持ちでした。コーヒーを飲みながら、窓から通りを眺めました。
sentences.romanized: Yuki-san wa hohoende, totemo shiawase na kimochi deshita. Kōhī wo nominagara, mado kara tōri wo nagamemashita.
literal: コーヒーを飲みながら
sentences.grammarNote:
**気持ち** (kimochi) = feeling/sensation: 幸せな気持ち (a happy feeling), 気持ちがいい (feels good), 気持ちが悪い (feels bad/disgusting). **〜な気持ちでした** = was in a ~ feeling — **な**-adjective modifying 気持ち: 嬉しい気持ち, 不安な気持ち.
**眺める** (nagameru) = to gaze at / to take in (a view) — different from **見る** (to look at, intentional) or **見える** (to be visible). **眺める** implies a leisurely, contemplative gaze: 海を眺める (gaze at the sea), 夕日を眺める (watch the sunset). **窓から** = from the window — **から** marks the origin point: 窓から見える (visible from the window).

sentences.base_language: Whatever the result, she knew she had already given her best.
sentences.target_language: 結果がどうであれ、彼女はもう最善を尽くしたとわかっていました。
sentences.romanized: Kekka ga dō de are, kanojo wa mō saizen wo tsukushita to wakatte imashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜がどうであれ** = whatever ~ may be — a concessive phrase expressing 'regardless of how ~': 結果がどうであれ (whatever the result). Other patterns: **〜ても** (even if): 失敗しても (even if she fails), **〜にかかわらず** (regardless of, formal): 結果にかかわらず (regardless of the result).
**最善を尽くす** (saizen wo tsukusu) = to do one's best, lit. 'to exhaust one's best' — a set expression. **尽くす** (tsukusu) = to exhaust/use up completely: 力を尽くす (give all one's strength), 手を尽くす (try every means). **わかっていました** = knew (was in a state of knowing — **〜ている** for ongoing understanding).
